mappings.rationaleAI: African history's coverage of the Bantu expansion and Great Lakes kingdoms provides context for the geography and ethnography of Central Africa (A) and the development of Bantu states (B).
Africa's overview including the Bantu expansion and major empires provides broad context for Central African civilizations (A).
Stone Age developments in Africa including Early, Middle, and Later Stone Age periodization relate to the origins of Central African cultures in the Stone Age (B.1).
The Iron Age in sub-Saharan Africa relates to the technological context underlying the emergence and expansion of Bantu-speaking peoples (B.1).

The DRC as Central Africa's largest country, centered on the Congo Basin, relates to the geography of Central Africa (A), and the Kongo kingdom is directly relevant to the development of Bantu states (B.2).

The Atlantic slave trade's African origins and participation relate to the Kongo and other Central African kingdoms' interactions with European slave traders during the development of Bantu states (B.2).
Portuguese exploration and contact with the Kongo kingdom relates to European encounters with Central African Bantu states (B.2).
The Scramble for Africa's partition of the continent by European powers provides context for the endpoint of Central African independence around c. 1885 (A).
The post-classical period's geographic expansion of civilizations provides context for the development of Bantu states from c. ad 1400 (B.2).
Prehistory from first hominin tool use through the Stone and Iron Ages relates to the origins of Central African cultures in the Stone Age (B.1).
Swahili as a Bantu language of the Great Lakes region provides linguistic context for the Bantu-speaking peoples of Central Africa (A).
